# Questions in Livestream 230

Live Stream #230: Ten Things About One Thing

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=786" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:06</a> | Why didn't early alphabetic writers base their letters on the Egyptian uniliteral signs with the same sounds? | He says the reason is unknown and notes that other languages also borrowed scripts without preserving the source phonetic system. | He says there is no known reason, even though many Egyptian uniliteral signs would have seemed phonetically suitable. He compares it to Nubian Kerma borrowing Egyptian symbols without keeping Egyptian phonetic names and treats it as one of those language-formation choices where speakers simply changed the system. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=890" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:50</a> | What do you make of the marijuana-use claim at Tel Arad? | He is skeptical and jokes that contamination or later misuse of the site is more plausible than sensational conclusions. | He responds skeptically, suggesting the residue could be from later people or even excavators rather than ancient ritual use. He likens it to sensational claims about tobacco and cocaine on Egyptian mummies, which he attributes to modern contamination rather than evidence for New World products in ancient Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:18</a> | Would modern contamination and ancient use have been identifiable as different test results? | Not necessarily; he says sample sizes and residue concentrations can be too small to distinguish ancient from modern contamination without further testing. | He says the residues are often so small that routine testing may not distinguish ancient use from modern contamination. He gives the example of vanilla-like residue in ancient Israelite pottery, where the test could not readily separate vanilla from compounds also present in oak, and says additional tests and sufficient residue concentrations would be needed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=1189" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:49</a> | Why would Pharaoh Neco change Eliakim's name to Jehoiakim and add a Yahweh element? | He suggests Neco likely needed an administrative way to distinguish this vassal from other rulers with El names, not that Neco cared about Yahweh. | He argues the change was probably administrative, since El names were common among Israelite and Canaanite rulers and Neco may have needed to keep vassals distinct. The Yahweh element need not imply Egyptian devotion to Yahweh; it likely made this particular vassal easier to identify within Egyptian bureaucracy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=1312" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:52</a> | Did you write today? | He says progress is progress, then urges viewers to contribute creatively because arts and writing can shape culture. | He says he personally had not been able to write for several days, but uses the question to encourage creative work broadly. Writing, poetry, novels, painting, sculpture, jewelry, and even book reviews matter because art and entertainment shape moral imagination and culture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=1417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:37</a> | Does sermon writing count as creative work? | Yes, if the pastor is doing the actual text work and writing rather than recycling canned sermon material. | He says sermon writing can change the culture of a congregation when pastors actually write their own sermons. He contrasts that with sermon books and prepackaged material, arguing that recycled sermons lack the same pastoral heart, direction, and work as a sermon prepared by the preacher. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=1636" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:16</a> | Is your fiction making an impact, and do non-Christians read it? | He says the answer is yes if non-Christians are reading it, because the goal is to reach outside the Christian bubble. | He says non-Christian readership itself is evidence of impact because fiction can function as outreach outside church settings. Pastors may minister inside the church, but he urges them not to neglect or discourage congregants whose creative work reaches outsiders. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=1892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:32</a> | How should Genesis 14:13's Hebrew reference to Abram be understood? | He thinks it is a corruption or later shaping of a term related to apiru/habiru, a class designation for sojourners, mercenaries, and outsiders. | He rejects the idea that the term simply means a descendant of Eber or a later Hebrew-language speaker. Instead, he thinks the word was related to apiru or habiru and later made more familiar in the Masoretic tradition, fitting Abram's status as a sojourner or outsider near Mamre. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=2121" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:21</a> | What is the best understanding of Adam being alone in Genesis 2:18 before the fall? | He says creation was not yet perfected; Adam being alone was not evil, but it was not the completed good God was moving creation toward. | He distinguishes morally neutral, unfinished creation from evil. In his reading, Genesis 1 shows God progressively ordering and completing creation, so Adam's aloneness is "not good" because creation is not yet in its intended completed state, not because sin has entered. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=2405" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:05</a> | Why does Genesis 14:18 mention bread and wine? | He treats it as basic hospitality: bread and wine were common staples to offer important guests, especially an armed visitor like Abram. | He says bread and wine were ordinary staples that people of moderate means would have on hand, and wine could be mixed with water to make it safer to drink. Melchizedek's offering is therefore basic hospitality to Abram and his armed party, not necessarily a coded ritual explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=2623" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:43</a> | What are the best and worst reasons you have heard for reading or not reading the Bible? | He says the best reason to read is to know God better; the worst is to trip up others, while confusion can be a reasonable reason someone hesitates to read. | He names knowing God better as the best reason to read Scripture and trying to embarrass biblically ignorant Christians as the worst. For not reading, he says confusion can be understandable when people lack cultural background, but refusing to read because one might need to change is the worst motive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=2729" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:29</a> | If Genesis 14:20 does not name Abram as giving the tithe, how do we know Abram gave it? | He says the context makes Abram the sensible giver, since Melchizedek giving Abram a tenth of Salem would not fit the scene. | He notes that the verse has ambiguous pronouns, but the scene narrows the likely giver to Abram or Melchizedek. Melchizedek giving Abram a tenth of Salem or all he has makes little sense, while Abram giving a tenth through Melchizedek as priest of God Most High fits the context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=2899" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:19</a> | Does God send visions when he believes they are necessary? | Yes, but he says such visions must be tested by Scripture and are usually personal rather than new revelation for the whole church. | He says God can still send visions, angels, messages, or prophecies, but none of them rise to the level of Scripture. Any claimed message must be consistent with the faith already received in Scripture, and he thinks such messages are more likely personal than new revelation for the whole church. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=3161" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:41</a> | How long would Abraham have been with Abimelech for Genesis 20:17-18 to be noticed? | He says at least nine months and probably a few years, since a broad fertility stoppage would need time to become evident. | He reads the passage as saying Abimelech's household was unable to bear children because of Sarah. For that condition to be noticed across wives and maidservants, Abraham would need to have stayed at least a full pregnancy cycle and probably long enough for repeated failed births to become obvious. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=3366" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:06</a> | Which Bible passage makes you facepalm because of secondhand embarrassment? | He names Jephthah's speech in Judges, saying it is full of errors and shows how ignorant Jephthah is. | He points to Jephthah's speech or letter to the Ammonites in Judges, saying the narrative already presents Jephthah as poorly formed and surrounded by "empty" men. The embarrassment comes from how much Jephthah gets wrong while speaking as though he understands Israel's history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=3478" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:58</a> | In Genesis 20:12, were Abraham and Sarah half-siblings or is another kinship custom involved? | He says they could be half-siblings, but Hebrew kinship terms can also cover more distant relations such as grandparents, aunts, uncles, or cousins. | He says the half-sibling reading is possible, but not the only option because Hebrew family terms are broader than modern English ones. Father can mean grandfather, sister can include other female relatives, and brother can include uncle or cousin, so Abraham may be speaking truly within ancient kinship categories while still evading the full situation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=3689" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:29</a> | Is there evidence that Epiphany or Christmas was celebrated in the first, second, or third century AD? | He says there is no first-century evidence, some late second-century evidence, and definite evidence by the third century, especially around Epiphany. | He says he cannot cite the exact fathers from memory, but his recollection is no first-century evidence, some evidence by the late second century, and definite evidence by the third. He advises looking especially for Epiphany, since Christmas observance appears to have been keyed to Epiphany in the early evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=3771" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:51</a> | In Genesis 19:7, is the wickedness sexual relations or mistreating guests? | He says the context points to sexual wrongdoing, because Lot's proposed substitution of his daughters would not answer a mere hospitality issue. | He argues that Lot's response in the next verse interprets the offense: offering his daughters would not solve a simple breach of hospitality. He also points to the use of "know" in a sexual sense elsewhere in Genesis and says Lot is a good host but a terrible father in this scene. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=3982" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:22</a> | What separates the clan of Enoch from the patriarch Enoch, and why is Enoch not a rapture archetype? | He says Enoch is the notable final progenitor of the clan, and his being taken is a poor rapture archetype because it is not a judgment-removal text. | He treats Enoch as the final and famous progenitor figure by whom the clan is identified, so the clan ends when Enoch is taken. He rejects the rapture typology because Enoch's removal is not presented as the wicked being judged or the righteous escaping an end-time tribulation pattern. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=4180" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:40</a> | How is the rapture not really biblical, and is it opposite to the common depiction? | He argues the common prooftexts describe judgment first, so the ones "taken" are the wicked being removed, not believers escaping beforehand. | He walks through common rapture prooftexts such as the wheat and tares and argues they do not describe believers being removed before judgment. In his reading, the wicked are gathered for judgment first, so popular rapture teaching reverses the direction of the imagery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=4868" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:08</a> | In Genesis 19:5, why did the men of Sodom act that way? | He gives no complex explanation beyond saying they were wicked people doing evil things. | He does not give a more elaborate motive for the men of Sodom. His answer is that people do evil things because wickedness abounds, so the text does not require a deeper psychological explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=4914" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:54</a> | Does Lot sitting at the gate in Genesis 19:1 imply he was well esteemed? | It implies he held some elder or dispute-settling role, though he says that does not necessarily mean moral esteem. | He explains that city gates were places where elders settled petty legal disputes and sometimes where a king might judge cases. Lot sitting there suggests some civic or judicial role, but that does not automatically mean the city respected him morally or that he was righteous in their eyes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=5241" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:21</a> | How do you reconcile the different genealogies of Jesus in Matthew and Luke? | He follows the common view that one line runs through Joseph and the other through Mary. | He says Matthew gives the legal line through Joseph, while Luke gives the genealogical line through Mary. Together, in his view, the genealogies reinforce Jesus as son of David through both legal inheritance and biological descent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=5357" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:17</a> | Why is Abraham so insistent that the three visitors stay in Genesis 18? | He treats it as a strong act of hospitality in the setting just before the Sodom episode. | He identifies the visitors as a theophany with two angelic figures and says Abraham expects to receive a blessing. The visit confirms Sarah's coming son and introduces the Sodom judgment, so the hospitality scene matters because Abraham is hosting divine messengers at a decisive moment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=5621" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:41</a> | Besides Exodus, which biblical book is most important to you, and why? | He names Genesis, Isaiah, and Jeremiah as his main candidates for ritual, messianic, and sacrificial-system reasons. | He does not choose one final book, but says Isaiah is crucial for messianic prophecy, Jeremiah for understanding the sacrificial system, and Genesis for ritual foundations. Genesis matters to him less for creation debates than for how it preserves ancient Near Eastern ritual patterns behind Israelite practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=5753" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:53</a> | Why were slaves circumcised in Genesis 17:13? | He says male servants were brought under the covenant household, so circumcision marked inclusion in that covenant order. | He connects Genesis 17 to household participation in Passover: male servants born in or bought into the household had to be circumcised because everyone in the household was required to participate. Without circumcision, the household would be left with disobedience hanging over it, so there were no exemptions for male household servants. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=6076" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:16</a> | Can we assume Adam was never immortal, since Genesis 3:22 says he had not yet eaten from the tree of life? | He says the passage is debated, but it at least leaves room for Adam not yet possessing immortality. | He says theologians have long debated the passage, with some arguing Adam and Eve had provisional immortality and could have become immortal through the tree of life. He thinks Genesis 3:22 at least supports the possibility that they did not begin with full bodily immortality, though he notes other church fathers took different views. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=6305" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:05</a> | Where does the idea of not seeing God and living come from, if Genesis 16 comes earlier chronologically? | He answers by treating Hagar's experience as a mediated theophany rather than the same kind of direct vision later warnings address. | He notes that Hagar herself expresses surprise at seeing God and living, so the idea already predates Exodus 33. The distinction he draws is between a theophany in a similacrum or self-limited form and seeing the full glory of God's face, which would destroy the viewer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=6702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:42</a> | Is it possible that Moses, Aaron, and Miriam were not literal siblings but members of the same Amramite clan? | He says it is possible and warns against being too dogmatic that the sibling terms must mean literal siblings. | He says the clan reading is viable and not especially problematic. Because kinship and clan language can be broader than modern literal sibling language, he cautions against making the literal-sibling interpretation more certain than the text requires. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=6764" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:44</a> | What does Genesis 16:12 mean? | He says Ishmael is being described as stubborn, combative, untamable, and living east of his brothers in the wilderness regions. | He interprets the "wild donkey" image as a picture of stubbornness, pride, brawling, and resistance to being tamed. Living east of his brothers points to desert regions such as Jordan, Moab, Edom, and Ammon, where Ishmael's descendants are portrayed as wilderness people in conflict with others. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=6900" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:00</a> | With increasing digital ID requirements, what is the possibility of a mark of the beast? | He warns against newspaper prophecy and says the mark is more likely tied to allegiance to the beast's teaching than simply to a digital ID. | He says the mark may involve buying and selling, but the forehead-and-hand language echoes Exodus language about God's word on the hand and forehead. For that reason he thinks the mark is primarily about allegiance, belief, and action rather than assuming every digital identification system is the literal mark. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=7517" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:17</a> | Why does Sarah blame Abraham in Genesis 16:4-5? | He says Sarah's scheme backfires and she turns the resulting humiliation against Abraham. | He stresses that the plan was Sarah's idea and Hagar was Sarah's servant, yet Sarah blames Abraham once Hagar despises her. Abram's reply that Hagar remains in Sarah's power shows, in his reading, that Abraham cannot simply punish Hagar as though she were his own servant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=7889" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:29</a> | How do people who read Genesis 6:3 as a lifespan limit interpret Genesis 25:7 and Numbers 33:39? | He says they usually see the long patriarchal ages as a post-flood diminishing curve toward a 120-year maximum, though he prefers the countdown-to-flood reading. | He explains that the lifespan-limit reading was once common among twentieth-century creationists, who used it to explain decreasing patriarchal ages after the flood. Abraham's 175 years and Aaron's 123 years would be placed on a diminishing curve, while he prefers reading the 120 years as the countdown from God's statement to the flood. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=8282" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:02</a> | What biblical scene do artists always get wrong that you would like to see done accurately? | He says Ruth, especially because she is often depicted as a young girl when the story presents her as an adult widow. | He wants Ruth depicted as an adult widow rather than an innocent teenage girl. He argues the text presents her as a woman who had already been married and whose family situation makes better sense if she is past the age artists often imagine for her. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=8758" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:58</a> | Which Aram is meant in Genesis 22:21? | He says the options include an ordinary person named Aram, ethnography, or retrojection, and he leans toward an ordinary person named Aram. | He notes several possibilities: Aram could be a personal name, an ethnographic reference, or a later geographic retrojection. Because Arameans as a people enter the region much later, after the collapse of Mitanni, he is cautious about reading the verse as evidence for Arameans in Abraham's own time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=9033" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:33</a> | Does the plural form *elohim* in Genesis 1 refer to God and the sons of God together? | He says no and treats it there as a majestic plural rather than a true numerical plural. | He says the determining feature is verb agreement: a true plural subject takes a plural verb, while a majestic plural is plural in form but singular in number. In Genesis 1:1, the verb for "created" is singular, so *elohim* is interpreted as referring to God singularly, not God plus the sons of God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=9291" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:51</a> | In Genesis 24:47, did the ring really go into Rebekah's nose? | Yes; he says it is a nose ring. | He says the wording means a nose ring and that such jewelry was customary in the ancient Near East. He also describes bracelets and nose rings as forms of betrothal jewelry, not merely decorative items unrelated to marriage negotiations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=10236" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:36</a> | Who is the commander in Joshua 5:13-15? | He treats the figure as a Christophany because the command to remove sandals marks the ground as holy. | He says the title "captain of the host of the Lord" could raise Michael as a possibility, but the holy-ground command rules that out. Since an angelic servant would not make the ground holy in that way, he identifies the figure as a Christophany, the pre-incarnate Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=10426" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:46</a> | Does Genesis 25:20 show that Abraham was Aramean? | He says the Aramean language in that verse applies to Rebekah's family setting, not to Abraham as an ethnic reclassification. | He says the references to Paddan-Aram and Arameans are retrojections using later familiar terminology for the reader. They describe Rebekah's family region in terms that fit a later period, not Abraham's ethnicity, and he still identifies Abraham as Amorite rather than Aramean. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=10967" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:47</a> | How should we study the book of Joshua? | He says Joshua is didactic, organized from good tribes to bad tribes, and should be read as teaching Israel through examples of obedience and failure. | He says Joshua is arranged as a teaching book, moving from good examples such as Judah to bad examples such as Dan. The same pattern appears in Judges, so Joshua should be read as instruction through tribal successes and failures, warning Israel about the results of staying faithful or going its own way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=11192" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:32</a> | How did Israelite identity become distinct from Amorite identity? | He treats the Exodus as the nation-forming event that turned the sons of Jacob and the mixed multitude into Israel. | He says the Israelites in Egypt still spoke an Amorite-related dialect and lived in an Amorite cultural environment at Avaris. The Exodus gathered the sons of Jacob together with a mixed multitude of other peoples, creating a new melting pot whose covenant and historical experience formed Israelite identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=11649" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:09</a> | If Abraham was Amorite, would that make his origins Hamitic through Genesis 10? | He says no, because Genesis 10 is ethnography rather than strict genealogy; Amorites are culturally assigned there, not genealogically derived from Ham. | He warns not to use Genesis 10 as though it were a strict family tree. In his view, Amorites are genealogically Semitic but ethnographically placed with Ham because Genesis 10 assigns cultural types and associations, so confusing ethnography with genealogy produces wrong conclusions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=11719" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:19</a> | What statutes and laws did Abraham keep in Genesis 26:5? | He says the covenant requirements Abraham personally received, especially circumcision, are part of the answer. | He says the clearest statute Abraham received from God was the covenant of circumcision, including circumcising his household. Beyond that, Abraham appears familiar with and sometimes quotes Mesopotamian laws, but the exact divine commandments meant in Genesis 26:5 are not fully specified. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=11890" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:10</a> | How should Matthew 19:23-26 be understood in context? | He reads it as a warning about the spiritual danger of riches, not as a wooden slogan detached from the disciples' panic in context. | He begins with the rich young ruler asking what good thing he must do for eternal life, which he says is already the wrong works-oriented question. Jesus exposes the man's attachment to wealth, and the disciples' alarm shows the point is not a detached saying but a serious warning that riches create spiritual danger only God can overcome. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=12664" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:04</a> | What are treasures in heaven? | He says the greatest treasure is relational: face time with Christ, not gold or material wealth. | He says heaven's gold, pearls, and materials are not the real treasure because they are common building materials there. The scarce and therefore precious treasure is time in the presence of the incarnate Christ, especially for those such as the apostles and martyrs who are granted greater nearness to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=12963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:03</a> | Could time be experienced differently after the resurrection? | He allows that it could be somewhat different, but says Scripture still presents causal sequence, so one should not rely on that speculation. | He allows some difference in resurrected experience but says Scripture still presents sequence and causality. Because the point about storing up treasure in heaven remains, he says not to bet on altered time as a way around the warning about seeking heavenly treasure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=13022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:02</a> | Does Genesis 27:29 imply Isaac and Rebekah had more children? | He says the language is ambiguous and likely prophetic, referring to peoples and descendants rather than proving extra named sons. | He says "brothers" and "mother's sons" may refer to descendants and nations, since the blessing itself is prophetic and Jacob personally did not see nations bow to him. It could also include broader family lines, so the phrase does not by itself prove Isaac and Rebekah had additional named sons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=13265" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:41:05</a> | Was Rahab simply a prostitute, or are we getting her role wrong? | He says she functioned more like an innkeeper or madam whose establishment also made sense as a place for clandestine political activity. | He explains ancient inns as households or establishments where a flat fee could include room, food, drink, and sexual companionship, often arranged by the innkeeper. That makes Rahab more like an innkeeper or madam, and such inns were natural places for travel, intelligence, and political plotting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=13616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:46:56</a> | Why did Esau care about not being blessed in Genesis 27:34? | He says the blessing was about divine approval, clan leadership, and public respect, unlike the birthright, which was mainly property. | He distinguishes the birthright as the double inheritance share from the blessing as Isaac's recommendation of divine favor and clan leadership. Esau may not have cared much about managing land or flocks, but he cared deeply about status, respect, and being recognized as clan chief. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=13913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:53</a> | Is Rahab's inn why city authorities came to her specifically? | Yes; he says an inn was exactly where suspicious foreign visitors would likely go. | He says the authorities would naturally check the innkeeper first when foreign men entered the city and disappeared. An inn was the expected place for travelers, spies, or suspicious outsiders to seek lodging. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=13934" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:14</a> | Would Esau's birthright sale have mattered legally without Isaac's approval? | Yes; he says the sale could still matter because inheritance property could be transferred and settled after Isaac's death. | He says a birthright was property and could be sold, with the transfer applied when the inheritance was divided after Isaac's death. Isaac's approval would not be decisive because Esau sold property rights, while the separate blessing concerned divine help, respect, and clan leadership. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=14318" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:58:38</a> | What life lessons did David and Kiara learn from their parents and grandparents? | He gives a personal answer, saying he mostly received shallow platitudes rather than deep life lessons from his parents. | He answers mainly from his own family, saying his grandfather's advice was humorous and his father tended toward shallow aphorisms rather than useful life instruction. He says his parents expected him to learn life lessons on his own, while Kiara's experience may have differed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=14480" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:01:20</a> | What advice do you have for Christians dealing with existential dread? | He says the cure is understanding human significance before God rather than measuring worth by cosmic size. | He says existential dread comes from thinking humans are meaningless and that nothing they do matters. His answer is that one person can change history, citing figures such as Martin Luther, Martin Luther King Jr., and above all Christ, so human actions have real and eternal significance before God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=14876" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:07:56</a> | Does mentioning the Westminster Confession reliably indicate your denomination? | He says no; citing it does not function as a denomination reveal. | He says he cites the Westminster Confession because it is a thoughtful document, not because it necessarily identifies his denomination. He also mentions drawing from other confessions and church fathers, so the citation is a useful source rather than a denominational clue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=14945" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:09:05</a> | Why couldn't Isaac simply bless Esau differently? | He says the chief blessing concerned singular clan leadership and could not just be duplicated. | He says Isaac could not give the same kind of blessing twice because clan leadership and the divine stamp of approval are singular. Once Jacob received the leadership blessing, Esau's only remaining way to become clan chief would be violent removal, which is why the scene becomes so dangerous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=15035" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:10:35</a> | When was Esau's Genesis 27:40 prophecy fulfilled? | He ties it to Moab's later sword-based existence, service under Omri, and revolt under Mesha. | He reads the prophecy as pointing to Esau's descendants being pushed into Moab, living by the sword, serving Israel, and eventually breaking free. He identifies service under King Omri and revolt under King Mesha as the historical fulfillment of those elements. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=15157" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:12:37</a> | Why would Rebekah lose both sons in one day in Genesis 27:45? | He says Esau would have needed to kill Jacob and Isaac to become clan chief, and the attempt could also get Esau killed by the servants. | He says Esau's plan to kill Jacob would not solve the leadership issue unless Isaac was also removed. If Esau failed or attacked Isaac, the household servants would likely kill Esau, so Rebekah could lose Jacob to murder and Esau to retaliation in the same crisis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=15250" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:14:10</a> | Is Islam a particularly concerning religion or just one fallacy among many? | He says it is concerning in the West because secularism creates a religious vacuum, though he cautions against weak anti-Islam arguments. | He says Islam is a serious concern in the West because secularism has displaced Christianity and left a religious vacuum that Islam can fill. At the same time, he urges Christians to use responsible, evidence-based arguments, rejecting weak claims such as Muhammad mythicism and cautioning against overstating points about Mecca. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=15766" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:22:46</a> | Why does Laban say everything is his in Genesis 31:43? | He says Laban treated his daughters, grandchildren, and flocks as extensions of his own clan property and could not let go. | He says Laban had no sons and treated his daughters, grandchildren, flocks, and even property already divided with Jacob as still belonging to him. Laban's statement shows a man unable to let go of the clan that had effectively left with Jacob, even though he had little he could do short of violence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=16062" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:27:42</a> | What made angels stand out from men in the Bible? | He says angels in similacrum form could look human unless their glory showed; their native form is different and not reproductive flesh. | He distinguishes angels' humanlike similacrum form from their native spiritual form. In humanlike form they can be indistinguishable unless glory is revealed, but they are not flesh-and-bone humans and cannot reproduce; native forms may be perceived through culturally shaped imagery because the reality is difficult for human perception to process. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=16343" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:32:23</a> | Please explain Genesis 32:24-30. | He says Jacob wrestles a divine figure before meeting Esau, receives a wound and a blessing, and is renamed Israel. | He places the wrestling scene just before Jacob faces Esau, when Jacob fears he is doomed after leaving Laban. The divine figure wrestles fairly, wounds Jacob's hip, blesses him, and renames him Israel, while Jacob recognizes that he has seen God and lived. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=16654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:37:34</a> | Does crushing the serpent's head in Genesis 3:15 refer to Jesus on the cross or to Satan's final destruction? | He says the heel-bruising happens at the cross, while the serpent's decisive defeat is secured by the resurrection. | He says Christian interpretation of Genesis 3:15 comes through early church teaching rather than an explicit later biblical explanation. The serpent bruises Christ's heel at the cross, but the resurrection is the decisive crushing of the serpent's head, leaving Satan defeated and only delaying the final outcome. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=16980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:43:00</a> | Does Genesis 33:20 mean Jacob identified Yahweh as El? | He says the patriarchs knew God through El titles before the name Yahweh was introduced through Moses, with later Yahweh references retrojected in the text. | He says Jacob's altar name identifies God as the God of Israel, and the patriarchs knew God through El titles such as El Elyon and El Shaddai. The divine name Yahweh is introduced with Moses in his view, while earlier Yahweh references are textual retrojections for later readers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=17105" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:45:05</a> | Do you think Randy Alcorn's book *Heaven* is accurate? | He has not read it, so he will not judge it, but says detailed books about heaven usually raise yellow flags for him. | He refuses to evaluate the book because he has not read it. He only says that books describing heaven in extreme detail tend to make him suspicious, so he would need to inspect the actual claims before making a judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=17168" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:46:08</a> | What does "in Israel" mean in Genesis 34:7? | He says the Dinah incident is framed as a grave clan violation that threatened tribal honor and security, not merely private wrongdoing. | He explains the phrase in the setting of clan honor and vendetta. Violating a woman from another clan could trigger total interclan warfare because failure to respond would mark the clan as weak and unable to protect its women, so the offense is framed as a public violation within Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=17718" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:55:18</a> | Did God force Pharaoh against his will by hardening his heart? | He says no; Pharaoh first hardens his own heart twice, and only then does God harden it. | He says the text first has Pharaoh refusing to release Israel and hardening his own heart. God's hardening comes after Pharaoh's own resistance, so he rejects readings that make God force an otherwise willing Pharaoh against his will from the start. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=17796" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:56:36</a> | In Genesis 34:29, what would Jacob's sons have done with the women and children? | He says women and girls would likely become slaves, concubines, or wives, while male posterity would be killed under clan vendetta logic. | He says female captives would likely become slaves, concubines, or wives, while male children and men would be killed to prevent future vendetta. He presents this as descriptive of Bronze Age clan warfare rather than as moral approval of the practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=17955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:59:15</a> | Would other Hivite tribes have wanted revenge after the Genesis 34 attack? | He says no; once the vendetta was satisfied, other clans would recognize the rules of clan honor and stay out of it. | He says other Hivite clans would know why the attack happened and would treat the vendetta as settled once the offending clan was destroyed. In his view, clan societies had unwritten but rigid honor rules, and outside clans would not defend a clan that had brought this kind of retaliation on itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fsOH2nJfj7o?t=18251" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:04:11</a> | In Genesis 1, is God creating order out of chaos, chaos out of order, or starting from order? | He says none of those exactly: Genesis presents unformed, empty potential, not chaos, and God gives it form, purpose, and goodness. | He reads "formless and void" as unformed, blank, and empty rather than chaotic or ordered. Genesis 1 is resumptive creation: God takes morally neutral, unformed potential and gives it form, purpose, order, and goodness, so the opposite of order here is lack of order rather than chaos. |
